quiz.title: SAT English: Unit 2 Quiz 11
quiz.subtitle: Command of Evidence in Humanities and Social Science Contexts (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation

questions.question:
Historians claim that the expansion of public education in the 19th century accelerated industrial growth primarily by creating a literate workforce, not by fostering civic engagement.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Region} & \text{Literacy Rate (%)} & \text{Factory Employment Growth (%)} \\ \hline\text{Region A} & 42 & 18 \\ \hline\text{Region B} & 67 & 54 \\ \hline\text{Region C} & 81 & 89 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this claim?
options: Civic participation metrics increased significantly in regions with the earliest public-school adoption.
Literacy rates and factory employment growth rose in parallel across all three regions studied.
Industrial output correlated more strongly with access to natural resources than with education levels.
Regions with higher literacy rates showed greater political stability during periods of economic change.
questions.explanation: Choice B directly supports the literate-workforce mechanism by showing parallel increases in literacy and industrial employment. Choice A emphasizes civic engagement, the alternative mechanism the claim downplays. Choice C introduces resource access, a confounding variable not central to the education claim. Choice D addresses political outcomes, irrelevant to the industrial-growth mechanism.

questions.question:
Economists propose that progressive taxation reduces income inequality primarily by redistributing resources through public services, not by discouraging high-income earning.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Country} & \text{Top Marginal Tax Rate} & \text{Gini Coefficient} \\ \hline\text{Country X} & 35\% & 0.42 \\ \hline\text{Country Y} & 52\% & 0.31 \\ \hline\text{Country Z} & 68\% & 0.24 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this proposal?
options: Public spending on education and healthcare increased proportionally with tax revenue in higher-tax countries.
High-income earners in countries with top rates above 60% reported reduced work hours and entrepreneurial activity.
Income inequality declined more rapidly in countries that combined progressive taxation with strong labor protections.
Tax compliance rates remained stable across all three countries despite differences in marginal rates.
questions.explanation: Choice B challenges the redistribution mechanism by showing behavioral responses that could reduce the tax base, suggesting disincentives may offset redistribution gains. Choice A directly supports the proposed public-services mechanism. Choice C describes policy complementarity, which doesn't isolate the taxation mechanism. Choice D addresses compliance, not the inequality-reduction pathway.

questions.question:
Sociologists claim that social media algorithms amplify political polarization primarily by creating echo chambers, not by increasing exposure to opposing viewpoints that trigger reactive antagonism.

Which finding from platform data would most strengthen this claim?
options: Users who engaged primarily with ideologically aligned content showed stronger attitude polarization over time than those exposed to mixed-content feeds.
Exposure to cross-partisan content increased user engagement metrics but also elevated self-reported feelings of political frustration.
Algorithmic recommendation systems prioritized content with high engagement regardless of its ideological orientation.
Users who actively sought diverse perspectives reported greater political tolerance than those who did not.
questions.explanation: Choice A directly supports the echo-chamber mechanism by showing polarization intensifies with ideologically homogeneous exposure. Choice B emphasizes reactive antagonism, the alternative mechanism the claim downplays. Choice C describes algorithmic design without testing its polarizing effect. Choice D addresses user agency, not the algorithm-driven mechanism.

questions.question:
Political scientists argue that proportional representation electoral systems increase party-system fragmentation primarily by lowering entry barriers for small parties, not by encouraging voter preference diversification.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Electoral System} & \text{Effective Number of Parties} & \text{Voter Preference Diversity Index} \\ \hline\text{First-Past-Post} & 2.1 & 3.4 \\ \hline\text{Mixed-Member} & 3.8 & 3.6 \\ \hline\text{Proportional Rep.} & 5.9 & 3.5 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this argument?
options: The effective number of parties rose sharply after electoral reform while preference diversity remained stable.
Small parties gained legislative seats in proportional systems at rates disproportionate to their vote share.
Voter preference diversity increased significantly in regions following the adoption of proportional representation.
Voter turnout increased in proportional-representation systems compared to majoritarian systems.
questions.explanation: Choice C challenges the entry-barrier mechanism by showing preference diversification rising alongside fragmentation, suggesting voter behavior—not institutional rules—drives the effect. Choice A directly supports the proposed entry-barrier mechanism. Choice B describes seat-vote disproportionality, consistent with the claim. Choice D addresses participation, not the party-system mechanism.

questions.question:
Anthropologists propose that long-distance trade networks accelerate cultural diffusion primarily by facilitating the exchange of ideas alongside goods, not by increasing population mobility between regions.

Which archaeological finding would most strengthen this proposal?
options: Genetic studies showed increased admixture between populations connected by major trade routes during the same period.
Settlement patterns shifted toward trade hubs, suggesting economic incentives drove population movement.
Languages in trade-connected regions showed greater lexical borrowing than those in isolated communities.
Artifacts bearing foreign stylistic elements appeared in regions with documented trade links but no evidence of population migration.
questions.explanation: Choice D directly supports the idea-exchange mechanism by showing cultural diffusion without population movement, isolating trade as the transmission pathway. Choice A emphasizes population mobility, the alternative mechanism the proposal downplays. Choice B describes economic migration, which doesn't isolate cultural transmission. Choice C addresses linguistic change but doesn't distinguish idea exchange from population movement.

questions.question:
Geographers claim that climate-induced migration reshapes settlement patterns primarily by pushing populations toward resource-rich areas, not by pulling them toward existing urban infrastructure.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Migration Driver} & \text{Destination: Resource-Rich Areas (%)} & \text{Destination: Urban Centers (%)} \\ \hline\text{Drought} & 68 & 32 \\ \hline\text{Flooding} & 71 & 29 \\ \hline\text{Temperature Extremes} & 64 & 36 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this claim?
options: Resource-rich destinations showed higher migrant retention rates than urban centers over five-year follow-up periods.
Climate vulnerability indices correlated strongly with out-migration rates across all studied regions.
Urban centers that invested in climate-resilient infrastructure attracted more migrants than those that did not.
Migrants from climate-affected regions disproportionately selected destinations with pre-existing social networks and job markets.
questions.explanation: Choice D challenges the resource-push mechanism by showing migration driven by social/economic pull factors, suggesting urban infrastructure—not resource availability—guides destination choice. Choice A directly supports the proposed resource-attraction mechanism. Choice B describes climate vulnerability patterns, consistent with the claim. Choice C emphasizes urban adaptation, which doesn't isolate the resource-versus-infrastructure comparison.

questions.question:
Social psychologists hypothesize that group identity strengthens cooperation primarily by enhancing in-group trust, not by increasing out-group competition that motivates internal cohesion.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Condition} & \text{In-Group Trust Score} & \text{Cooperation Rate (%)} \\ \hline\text{Neutral Identity} & 4.2 & 38 \\ \hline\text{Salient In-Group} & 6.8 & 71 \\ \hline\text{Salient Out-Group Threat} & 5.1 & 69 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most strengthen this hypothesis?
options: Cooperation rates rose with in-group trust scores while out-group threat perceptions remained constant across conditions.
Participants in the out-group-threat condition reported higher levels of intergroup anxiety than those in other conditions.
Trust scores correlated positively with cooperation rates across all experimental conditions and control groups.
In-group identification predicted cooperation more strongly in competitive than in cooperative task contexts.
questions.explanation: Choice A directly supports the in-group-trust mechanism by showing cooperation gains with stable threat perceptions, isolating trust as the causal pathway. Choice B emphasizes out-group anxiety, which doesn't isolate the in-group mechanism. Choice C provides correlational evidence but doesn't distinguish trust from threat effects. Choice D introduces task context, a confounding variable not central to the hypothesis.

questions.question:
Demographers claim that increased female education reduces fertility rates primarily by expanding economic opportunities, not by shifting cultural norms about family size.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Education Level} & \text{Female Labor Force Participation (%)} & \text{Avg. Children per Woman} \\ \hline\text{Primary or Less} & 34 & 5.2 \\ \hline\text{Secondary} & 58 & 3.1 \\ \hline\text{Tertiary} & 79 & 1.8 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this claim?
options: Fertility declines occurred equally among educated women in traditional and progressive cultural contexts.
Labor force participation increased significantly among women following expansions in secondary education access.
Cultural attitudes about ideal family size shifted toward smaller families in regions with rising female education.
Economic growth rates correlated more strongly with fertility decline than with female education levels alone.
questions.explanation: Choice C challenges the economic-opportunity mechanism by showing norm shifts accompanying education, suggesting cultural change—not just employment—drives fertility decline. Choice A directly supports the economic mechanism by showing consistent effects across cultural contexts. Choice B describes the education-employment link, consistent with the claim. Choice D introduces macroeconomic factors, which don't isolate the education mechanism.

questions.question:
Urban planners propose that expanding public transit access improves economic mobility primarily by reducing commute costs, not by connecting residents to higher-wage job clusters.

Which finding from longitudinal data would most strengthen this proposal?
options: Residents in newly connected neighborhoods showed increased employment in high-wage sectors compared to control areas.
Transit access improvements correlated with rising property values in previously underserved communities.
Commute times decreased more for low-income residents than for higher-income residents after service expansions.
Household transportation spending declined significantly following transit expansions while job proximity remained unchanged.
questions.explanation: Choice D directly supports the cost-reduction mechanism by showing spending declines without changes in job access, isolating affordability as the causal pathway. Choice A emphasizes job-cluster connections, the alternative mechanism the proposal downplays. Choice B addresses property values, a secondary outcome not central to mobility. Choice C describes differential time savings, which doesn't isolate cost versus access effects.

questions.question:
Comparative political scientists argue that decentralization fosters policy innovation primarily by enabling local experimentation, not by increasing inter-jurisdictional competition for residents and businesses.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline\text{Governance Structure} & \text{Policy Innovation Index} & \text{Inter-Jurisdictional Competition Score} \\ \hline\text{Centralized} & 2.4 & 3.1 \\ \hline\text{Moderately Decentralized} & 4.7 & 5.2 \\ \hline\text{Highly Decentralized} & 7.9 & 5.4 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which finding, if true, would most weaken this argument?
options: Highly decentralized regions showed greater variation in policy outcomes than centralized regions with uniform national standards.
Local governments in decentralized systems adopted innovative policies at higher rates than those in centralized systems.
Policy innovation scores correlated more strongly with competition metrics than with measures of local autonomy across jurisdictions.
Inter-jurisdictional competition increased modestly following decentralization reforms in most studied cases.
questions.explanation: Choice C challenges the local-experimentation mechanism by showing innovation linked more strongly to competition, suggesting inter-jurisdictional rivalry—not autonomy—drives the effect. Choice A describes outcome variation, consistent with the experimentation claim. Choice B directly supports the proposed local-autonomy mechanism. Choice D emphasizes competition increases but doesn't test their role relative to experimentation.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can accurately evaluate social-science evidence by distinguishing mechanism-specific support from true-but-irrelevant data.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on whether the evidence addresses the exact causal pathway stated in the claim, not just a related topic.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: eliminate options that describe plausible social phenomena but don't isolate the specific mechanism being tested.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Ask yourself: does this finding prove or disprove the precise causal relationship the researchers are proposing?
